Dear Nr. Mendelsohn: 
Re: 
Response to your letter of 
February 10, 1976 
Thank you for your letter of February 10, 1976, in which you 
pose several questions concerning the relative responsibilities of 
candidates and treasurers. 
Because you apparently seek only in-
formal advice, the Commission will not issue a formal opinion in 
response to your questions. 
I hope, however, that the following 
informal comments 1;vill be helpful. 
In response to your first question, the Political Reform Act 
contains no provision that addresses the respective decision-making 
authority of the candidate and committee treasurer. 
Section 84l00(b) 
provides that "No expenditure shall be made by or on behalf of a com-
mittee without the authorization of the treasurer or that of his desig-
nated agents," but this section is not intended to be determinative 
of \vhich party has the ultimate decision-making power with respect to 
an expenditure of committee funds. 
Rather, the section is designed 
to ensure that there will be someone who is responsible for expenditures 
of funds so that the disclosure prOvisions of the Act can be enforced. 
This responsibil~ty falls to the treasurer because he is the individual 
who must sign and verify all campaign statements. 
Ultimately, the 
determination of vlhich person or persons can decide whether to make an 
expenditure of committee funds, which the treasurer must then authorize, 
depends on the structure of the cowmittee. 
In response to questions #2 and

In response to question #4, it is clear that the treasurer has 
responsibility for the truth and completeness of campaign statements 
because, pursuant to Section 81004 of the Political Reform Act, he is 
required to verify that he has used all reasonable diligence in the 
statement's preparation and that, to the best of his knowledge, the 
statement is true and complete. 
However, pursuant to Section 84209 
of the Act, the candidate also must verify any campaign statement 
of a committee subhect to his control, and, accordingly, he too is 
subject to liability. 
The candidate's verification must be "in 
accordance tvith the provisions of Section 8l00 /! except th2.t it 
shall state that to the best of [the candic2.te's] knmJledge, the 
treasurer ... used all reasonable diligence in the preparation of 
the committee's statem~nt." 
Please contact me if you have any further questions.

sections.question: Several questions concerning the relative responsibilities of candidates and treasurers, including: (1) respective decision-making authority of candidate and committee treasurer; (2) whether a candidate who is not a signatory on a bank account can have access to bank records; (3) whether candidate must have access to treasurer's records; (4) responsibility for truth and completeness of campaign statements.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act contains no provision that addresses the respective decision-making authority of the candidate and committee treasurer. Section 84100(b) requires treasurer authorization for expenditures but is designed to ensure accountability for disclosure, not to determine ultimate decision-making power. The Act does not address whether a non-signatory candidate can access bank records (left to candidate and bank). The Commission has not determined whether candidate access to treasurer's records is required, though such access may be necessary. The treasurer has responsibility for truth and completeness pursuant to Section 81004, but the candidate must also verify campaign statements for committees subject to his control under Section 84209 and shares liability.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks clarification on the respective decision-making authority between candidates and committee treasurers under the Political Reform Act, including questions about candidate access to bank and treasurer records and liability for campaign statement accuracy.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not specifically address the decision-making authority between candidates and treasurers, leaving such matters to committee structure. The treasurer bears primary responsibility for campaign statement accuracy and verification, though candidates of controlled committees also bear verification responsibility and potential liability. Candidate access to bank and treasurer records is not mandated by the Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter; marked as not issuing formal opinion. Some document quality issues with scanning/OCR visible in both pages. Second page appears to be the incoming request. Main FPPC response is clear despite OCR artifacts. Questions and conclusions reconstructed from context and letter body.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Several questions concerning the relative responsibilities of candidates and treasurers, including: (1) respective decision-making authority of candidate and committee treasurer; (2) whether a candidate who is not a signatory on a bank account can have access to bank records; (3) whether candidate must have access to treasurer's records; (4) responsibility for truth and completeness of campaign statements.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act contains no provision that addresses the respective decision-making authority of the candidate and committee treasurer. Section 84100(b) requires treasurer authorization for expenditures but is designed to ensure accountability for disclosure, not to determine ultimate decision-making power. The Act does not address whether a non-signatory candidate can access bank records (left to candidate and bank). The Commission has not determined whether candidate access to treasurer's records is required, though such access may be necessary. The treasurer has responsibility for truth and completeness pursuant to Section 81004, but the candidate must also verify campaign statements for committees subject to his control under Section 84209 and shares liability.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal advice to Supervisor Mendelsohn regarding the respective responsibilities and authorities of candidates and committee treasurers under the Political Reform Act, clarifying that while the Act requires treasurer authorization and verification of campaign statements, decision-making authority depends on committee structure, and candidate access to financial records is not mandated by the Act.
